figures.caption: Figure 2: Overview of the Supervised Group Relative Policy Optimization (S-GRPO) framework. The core innovation is the Conditional Ground-Truth Injection (CGI) mechanism. During online exploration, the current policy generates a group of candidate trajectories (𝑂𝑔𝑒𝑛) that are evaluated by a strict reward verifier. If the verifier detects a complete exploratory failure (i.e., all 𝑟𝑖 = 0), CGI is triggered as a dynamic fallback (top path). It injects the expert ground-truth trajectory (𝑦𝑔𝑡 ) into the candidate pool to form a mixed group, guaranteeing a strongly positive advantage signal (𝐴𝑔𝑡 ≫ 0) that prevents optimization collapse. Conversely, if the policy successfully generates at least one valid trajectory (bottom path), the framework gracefully defaults to a standard GRPO update. This conditional routing seamlessly balances supervised imitation and autonomous multi-trajectory exploration within a single stage.

Table 1: Performance comparison of different post-training adaptation methods evaluating both Domain-Specific Ability and General-Purpose Ability. The results demonstrate the overarching superiority of our proposed single-stage S-GRPO framework. Notably, S-GRPO achieves domain-specific performance comparable to, or even exceeding, heavy teacher-forcing methods like Full Fine-Tuning (FFT) and the computationally expensive two-stage pipeline (SFT → GRPO). Crucially, unlike FFT which suffers from severe catastrophic forgetting (the "alignment tax"), S-GRPO successfully mitigates policy drift, robustly preserving the pre-trained model’s broad multimodal and instruction-following capabilities. The best results are highlighted in bold, and the second-best results are underlined. Our S-GRPO method and its ablation variant are shaded in gray.

Table 2: Ablation study on the control threshold 𝛿 for the Conditional Ground-Truth Injection (CGI) mechanism on the COCO Caption dataset. The results illustrate a clear trade-off between domain-specific adaptation and general-purpose capability retention. While a higher 𝛿 enforces a stricter reward condition that aggressively boosts domain learning, an excessively high value (e.g., 𝛿 = 1) degrades general capabilities. The highlighted row (𝛿 = 0.9) represents the optimal Pareto balance. Best results are in bold, and second-best are underlined.

Figure 3: Learning dynamics and reward progression of GRPO vs. S-GRPO. Facilitated by a Ground-Truth Injection mechanism, S-GRPO demonstrates a stable, monotonically increasing reward curve leading to robust convergence. In contrast, standard GRPO lacks explicit trajectory guidance and suffers from a severe "flatline" optimization collapse under a strict binary verifier, exhibiting only sporadic, negligible reward spikes without accumulating any meaningful policy gradients.
